ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): From the supplied summary the work addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis) and presents supervised-learning systems and a web service rather than a novel algorithmic or theoretical contribution. The paper primarily combines existing supervised models, multi-domain training and standard NLP components (topic/NER) and reports empirical gains on app-review and tweet benchmarks, but implementation details are undisclosed and no new method or formal analysis is proposed. Thus the contribution is largely engineering/empirical integration with limited methodological novelty and would not surprise an expert in the field. | B(2): The paper presents applied supervised-learning systems (App2Check/Tweet2Check) and empirical benchmarks in Italian and English, but does not introduce a new learning method or theoretical perspective; it mainly applies established supervised models and additional NLP modules to app reviews and tweets. The contribution appears engineering-oriented (service, web app, benchmarks) and compares extensively to 19 baselines, yet key implementation details are withheld and no novel algorithmic extension or formulation is described. From a practical-systems standpoint this is valuable, but in terms of methodological novelty it largely combines known techniques with limited originality.

reviewer_a.justification: From the supplied summary the work addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis) and presents supervised-learning systems and a web service rather than a novel algorithmic or theoretical contribution. The paper primarily combines existing supervised models, multi-domain training and standard NLP components (topic/NER) and reports empirical gains on app-review and tweet benchmarks, but implementation details are undisclosed and no new method or formal analysis is proposed. Thus the contribution is largely engineering/empirical integration with limited methodological novelty and would not surprise an expert in the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents applied supervised-learning systems (App2Check/Tweet2Check) and empirical benchmarks in Italian and English, but does not introduce a new learning method or theoretical perspective; it mainly applies established supervised models and additional NLP modules to app reviews and tweets. The contribution appears engineering-oriented (service, web app, benchmarks) and compares extensively to 19 baselines, yet key implementation details are withheld and no novel algorithmic extension or formulation is described. From a practical-systems standpoint this is valuable, but in terms of methodological novelty it largely combines known techniques with limited originality.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, benchmarks, web demo) are stated early. Presentation of evaluation (MF1, accuracy, per-class F1) and dataset descriptions is clear and figures/tables are reported as adequate. However, clarity is weakened by undisclosed engine implementation details and absent hyperparameter reporting, which leave important methodological sections underspecified. Minor wording/ambiguous-implementation issues remain but do not prevent understanding the core claims. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, multilingual evaluation, web service/demo) are stated early and followed by method and experiments, making the narrative easy to follow. Notation appears minimal and consistent for an applied system paper, and figures/tables summarizing datasets, baselines (19 tools + SentiStrength variants) and metrics (MF1/Acc/per-class F1) are informative. Writing quality is adequate with clear coverage of related work, though the omission of engine implementation details (non-disclosure) and lack of reported hyperparameters somewhat reduces reproducibility and leaves a few experimental-description gaps. From a practitioner’s perspective the presentation is strong with only minor clarity/reproducibility concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, benchmarks, web demo) are stated early. Presentation of evaluation (MF1, accuracy, per-class F1) and dataset descriptions is clear and figures/tables are reported as adequate. However, clarity is weakened by undisclosed engine implementation details and absent hyperparameter reporting, which leave important methodological sections underspecified. Minor wording/ambiguous-implementation issues remain but do not prevent understanding the core claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, multilingual evaluation, web service/demo) are stated early and followed by method and experiments, making the narrative easy to follow. Notation appears minimal and consistent for an applied system paper, and figures/tables summarizing datasets, baselines (19 tools + SentiStrength variants) and metrics (MF1/Acc/per-class F1) are informative. Writing quality is adequate with clear coverage of related work, though the omission of engine implementation details (non-disclosure) and lack of reported hyperparameters somewhat reduces reproducibility and leaves a few experimental-description gaps. From a practitioner’s perspective the presentation is strong with only minor clarity/reproducibility concerns.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper reports strong empirical comparisons against 19 state-of-the-art tools and provides multiple test sets (apps and tweets in Italian and English) with per-class F1 reported, which addresses baseline and per-class evaluation concerns. However, there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses, no hyperparameter reporting or statistical significance testing, and core engine implementation details are withheld (hurting reproducibility). Related work is adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (e.g. undisclosed implementation, missing neutral class in English tweets), but the lack of analyses explaining where gains originate keeps the experimental validation from being fully comprehensive. | B(3): The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive in breadth—multiple realistic app-review and tweet datasets in both Italian and English and comparisons against 19 research tools—so claims of strong empirical performance are fairly supported. However, there are no reported ablations or sensitivity analyses, hyperparameters are not provided, and key implementation details are withheld under non-disclosure, which substantially hinders reproducibility and understanding of what drives gains. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper acknowledges some limitations (e.g., missing neutral class in the English tweets), but the lack of released code/configuration and deeper error analysis keeps this from being a higher score from an applied-systems perspective.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports strong empirical comparisons against 19 state-of-the-art tools and provides multiple test sets (apps and tweets in Italian and English) with per-class F1 reported, which addresses baseline and per-class evaluation concerns. However, there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses, no hyperparameter reporting or statistical significance testing, and core engine implementation details are withheld (hurting reproducibility). Related work is adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (e.g. undisclosed implementation, missing neutral class in English tweets), but the lack of analyses explaining where gains originate keeps the experimental validation from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive in breadth—multiple realistic app-review and tweet datasets in both Italian and English and comparisons against 19 research tools—so claims of strong empirical performance are fairly supported. However, there are no reported ablations or sensitivity analyses, hyperparameters are not provided, and key implementation details are withheld under non-disclosure, which substantially hinders reproducibility and understanding of what drives gains. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper acknowledges some limitations (e.g., missing neutral class in the English tweets), but the lack of released code/configuration and deeper error analysis keeps this from being a higher score from an applied-systems perspective.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Important engineering details are missing: the paper explicitly states engine implementation details are withheld (NDA) and hyperparameters are not reported, so models cannot be reconstituted. The preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, and code is not reported as available; although benchmark data and a demo are said to be submitted, that alone does not enable reimplementation without model/config details. Evaluation metrics are precisely stated (Macro F1, Accuracy, per-class F1), but this is insufficient to make the work reproducible. | B(2): Important engineering details are withheld ("Engine implementation details are not disclosed") and hyperparameters/configurations are not reported, making exact replication infeasible. While datasets are largely described and some (Araújo et al.) are public and the authors state benchmark data/demo access were submitted, the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly documented and code is not mentioned. Evaluation metrics and procedures are defined (Macro F1, Accuracy, per-class F1), but without model/config/training specifics reproduction would be difficult.

reviewer_a.justification: Important engineering details are missing: the paper explicitly states engine implementation details are withheld (NDA) and hyperparameters are not reported, so models cannot be reconstituted. The preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, and code is not reported as available; although benchmark data and a demo are said to be submitted, that alone does not enable reimplementation without model/config details. Evaluation metrics are precisely stated (Macro F1, Accuracy, per-class F1), but this is insufficient to make the work reproducible.

reviewer_b.justification: Important engineering details are withheld ("Engine implementation details are not disclosed") and hyperparameters/configurations are not reported, making exact replication infeasible. While datasets are largely described and some (Araújo et al.) are public and the authors state benchmark data/demo access were submitted, the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly documented and code is not mentioned. Evaluation metrics and procedures are defined (Macro F1, Accuracy, per-class F1), but without model/config/training specifics reproduction would be difficult.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of baselines (19 state-of-the-art research tools via iFeel and SentiStrengthIta) and reports appropriate metrics including macro and per-class F1 on multiple held-out test sets, which is strong. However, fairness concerns remain because some competitors were evaluated through the iFeel platform (potentially not native Italian implementations), engine/hyperparameter details for the proposed systems are undisclosed, and the manuscript does not clearly state when prior methods perform comparably or better. Coverage of competing approaches is reported as adequate, but the comparison would be more convincing with full reproducibility details and explicit analysis of when baselines match or beat the new systems. | B(4): The paper runs broad empirical comparisons against 19 state-of-the-art tools (via the iFeel platform) plus SentiStrengthIta, and evaluates on multiple in-domain test sets with standard metrics (Macro-F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which aligns well with my emphasis on practical, broad evaluation. However, there are minor fairness and reproducibility concerns: use of iFeel to proxy tools without native Italian versions, undisclosed engine implementation/hyperparameters, and the English tweet set lacking a neutral class could affect comparability. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and benchmark scope is strong, so overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive or fully reproducible.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of baselines (19 state-of-the-art research tools via iFeel and SentiStrengthIta) and reports appropriate metrics including macro and per-class F1 on multiple held-out test sets, which is strong. However, fairness concerns remain because some competitors were evaluated through the iFeel platform (potentially not native Italian implementations), engine/hyperparameter details for the proposed systems are undisclosed, and the manuscript does not clearly state when prior methods perform comparably or better. Coverage of competing approaches is reported as adequate, but the comparison would be more convincing with full reproducibility details and explicit analysis of when baselines match or beat the new systems.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs broad empirical comparisons against 19 state-of-the-art tools (via the iFeel platform) plus SentiStrengthIta, and evaluates on multiple in-domain test sets with standard metrics (Macro-F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which aligns well with my emphasis on practical, broad evaluation. However, there are minor fairness and reproducibility concerns: use of iFeel to proxy tools without native Italian versions, undisclosed engine implementation/hyperparameters, and the English tweet set lacking a neutral class could affect comparability. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and benchmark scope is strong, so overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive or fully reproducible.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a useful, practical contribution—an effective multilingual sentiment-analysis system with artifacts and strong empirical results—so it has real-world impact for practitioners. However, the lack of novel theory/algorithms, limited implementation/hyperparameter disclosure, and reproducibility/dataset limitations constrain its influence on the research community. Balancing practical utility against limited scientific novelty and reproducibility yields a moderate impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: From my theoretical perspective this paper tackles a useful engineering problem (sentiment analysis for app reviews and tweets) and presents competitive supervised systems evaluated against 19 baselines with per-class F1 reported — which supports empirical adequacy. However, the contribution is primarily an applied system and web service with no new formal model, algorithmic innovation, or theoretical analysis, limited disclosure of implementation/hyperparameters, and no clear opening of substantial new research directions. Thus its likely impact is practical/niche rather than influential for how researchers think about or advance the field.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—robust sentiment analysis for app reviews and tweets in both Italian and English—and supplies usable artifacts (web service, user-friendly app, benchmark data and demo) that increase its real-world utility. The reported outperformance of 19 state-of-the-art tools on multiple multilingual benchmarks suggests practitioners could adopt these systems and that the results will influence applied work. However, the undisclosed engine implementation, absent code/hyperparameters, and some dataset limitations (e.g., missing neutral class in the English tweets) constrain reproducibility and broader scientific uptake, preventing a transformative (5) rating.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a strong applied contribution with extensive empirical comparisons and a useful demo, but both raise reproducibility concerns (undisclosed engine implementation, missing hyperparameters/code) and note limited novelty beyond an engineering/tool contribution. Given these strengths and the shared concerns, the paper fits well for an applied/demo track or a venue with stronger documentation requirements; it is borderline for a main conference without improved reproducibility, so a middle score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: While the paper addresses an NLP topic (sentiment analysis) and provides thorough empirical comparisons (19 baselines via iFeel, multiple datasets, per-class F1 and Macro F1), it reads primarily as an engineering/tool contribution without a novel model, algorithmic idea, or theoretical analysis—criteria I prioritize. Important methodological details are omitted (engine implementation undisclosed, hyperparameters not reported, code availability unclear), which weakens reproducibility and the kinds of insights top-tier NLP/ML venues expect. Given these factors, the work is more appropriate for an applied/demo track or an engineering-focused venue rather than a main conference paper at a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This is a strong applied submission that matches the venue audience: multilingual, cross-domain sentiment analysis with large app-review test sets (10k each language), tweet benchmarks, and comparisons to 19 state-of-the-art baselines; plus a web service and demo access which I value highly. However, reproducibility concerns (engine implementation undisclosed, no code or hyperparameters reported) and the English tweet set lacking a neutral class are minor issues that weaken its fit for a top-tier conference. Overall it is a good fit for ACL/EMNLP with those documentation/reproducibility improvements needed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents clear empirical claims and extensive experimental comparisons that generally support its findings, but they also concur that key reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters, engine implementation) and no statistical significance testing is reported; the English tweet set lacking a neutral class is another shared limitation. Given that these omissions undermine full technical validity and confidence in the reported wins, I rate soundness as mixed rather than fully strong.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and presents experiments comparing App2Check/Tweet2Check to 19 state-of-the-art tools on multiple datasets with macro and per-class F1 reported, which supports the main performance claims. However, important methodological omissions weaken technical validity: engine implementation details and hyperparameter settings are undisclosed (hurting reproducibility), and no statistical significance testing is reported to substantiate wins. The English tweet set lacking a neutral class is an additional limitation noted by the authors. Overall, the empirical evidence is promising but incomplete, so I rate technical soundness as mixed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear empirical claims and supports them with broad experimental comparisons (19 baselines via iFeel, SentiStrengthIta) on multiple app-review and tweet test sets in both Italian and English, using MF1/Acc and per-class F1—this addresses my emphasis on broad, cross-domain evaluation. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, engine implementation withheld) and no mention of statistical significance testing; additionally the English tweet set lacks a neutral class which weakens comparability. These are minor but important issues that do not overturn the experimental findings, so I rate the technical soundness as strong but not flawless.

main_claims: Presented App2Check and Tweet2Check, two supervised-learning based sentiment analysis tools for app reviews and tweets in Italian and English.
App2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on app reviews benchmarks.
Tweet2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on tweet benchmarks in both Italian and English.
The tools are provided as a web service and a user-friendly web application and benchmark data and a demo access are submitted with the paper.
_summary.methodology: The systems implement supervised learning predictive models trained on diverse language domains to produce 1–5 sentiment polarity scores (mapped to negative/neutral/positive) for app reviews and tweets, with additional NLP algorithms for tasks like topic/Named Entity extraction.
datasets: Test set A-ita (1000 Italian Candy Crush Saga comments, manually annotated HSQ)
Test set A-eng (1000 English Candy Crush Saga comments, manually annotated HSQ)
Test set B-ita (10,000 comments: 1,000 each from 10 popular Italian apps)
Test set B-eng (10,000 comments: 1,000 each from 10 popular English apps)
Italian tweets (3899 tweets from Araújo et al., 2016 / Sem 2016)
English tweets (1000 tweets randomly selected from same source)
baselines: 19 state-of-the-art research tools run via the iFeel platform
iFeel platform results used for tools without native Italian versions
metrics: Per-class F1 (F1 negative, F1 neutral if present, F1 positive)
limitations: Engine implementation details are not disclosed due to non-disclosure restrictions.
The English tweet dataset does not contain the neutral class.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a focus on formal models and theoretical analysis of learning algorithms. Extensive experience reviewing ACL/NeurIPS papers that introduce model/theory advances.
traits: Penalises papers that are primarily empirical/engineering without a clear theoretical contribution or novel model: will deduct points if no formal analysis, novel algorithmic idea, or theoretical insight is presented.

Requires rigorous experimental methodology: will deduct points if (a) fewer than 3 strong baselines (including simple baselines) are compared, (b) no ablation studies are shown, or (c) no statistical significance testing is reported.

Demands per-class evaluation and error analysis for multi-class sentiment: will deduct if only aggregate accuracy is reported and no confusion matrices/per-class F1 or class-imbalance handling is discussed.

Skeptical of claimed SOTA without substrate analysis: will penalise if improvements are small (<1-2% absolute) and not substantiated by analysis of where gains come from (data, pretraining, architecture, hyperparams).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the paper advances understanding (theory or model) and on methodological rigor; tends to give low scores to incremental, under-analysed empirical systems.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Systems Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher/engineer in applied NLP with experience building and deploying sentiment-analysis systems in industry and contributing to shared datasets and demos for ACL/EMNLP venues.
traits: Rewards papers that provide usable resources: will add positive weight if code, benchmark datasets, and a live demo/web service are publicly released and documented.

Values broad multilingual and cross-domain evaluation: will reward systems that show strong performance across multiple languages and realistic domains (e.g., tweets and app reviews) even if the model is engineering-heavy.
Favors applied impact and usability evidence: will increase score if there is a user-facing web application, API, or any user/field testing demonstrating practical utility.

Accepts engineering contributions without new theory provided experiments are comprehensive: will not penalise the absence of formal/theoretical novelty as long as baselines, datasets, and reproducible pipelines are provided.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasises empirical breadth, reproducibility, and real-world utility; tends to give higher scores to well-engineered, well-documented systems with released artifacts and demos.
